era_context: Early Dynastic Period III in Mesopotamia; Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty IV). Akkadian city-states consolidating. Indus Valley Civilization mature phase. Bronze Age in full development across Eurasia.

events.title: Old Kingdom Egypt Dynasty IV centralized rule
events.description: Dynasty IV pharaonic rule continued with centralized state authority. The pharaoh, understood as a living god, commanded the state apparatus, priesthood, and bureaucracy. The state's ability to mobilize resources for monumental construction demonstrates sophisticated economic organization and labor management.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Old Kingdom sites

events.certainty_note: Chronological uncertainty ±20–30 years for Old Kingdom dating.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization mature urban phase
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization was in its mature phase with fully developed urban centers. Archaeological evidence indicates sophisticated urban planning, standardized weights and measures, and extensive trade networks. The undeciphered script was in use on seals and pottery. The civilization's non-militaristic character suggests either peaceful internal organization or external security.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, and other Indus sites

events.certainty_note: Radiocarbon dating places mature Indus phase c. 2600–1900 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence for 2639 BCE. The year is archaeologically indistinguishable from surrounding years.
historiographic_note: This year is part of a stable period in documented civilizations. The historical record is sparse and relies on archaeological evidence and later compilations. No contemporary written records document 2639 BCE specifically. The five-year span from 2635–2639 BCE shows no documented change in the major civilizations of the period, reflecting either genuine stability or the limitations of the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Indus civilization in stable mature phase
